forTheKingIIBeginnerGuide.title: For The King II Beginner Guide
forTheKingIIBeginnerGuide.intro: For The King II is a turn-based roguelite tabletop RPG built around dice-inspired rolls, grid combat, and run-to-run progression. This beginner path focuses on the habits that keep a new party alive long enough to turn early chaos into actual progress.

steps.title: Build a Party Around Roles, Not Favorites
steps.goal: Cover frontline defense, ranged damage, magic damage, and support before chasing advanced unlocks.
details: Blacksmith protects the formation and anchors the front row.
Hunter handles Awareness-based physical damage and overworld scouting.
Scholar covers Intelligence-based magic, scrolls, and crowd control.
Herbalist keeps the group supplied with Party Heal and herb economy.
steps.tip: A balanced four-role party forgives more mistakes than four damage dealers stacked into the same wagon of bad decisions.

steps.title: Spend Focus Where Failure Hurts Most
steps.goal: Use Focus on dangerous attacks, key movement rolls, quest objectives, and rolls that protect party tempo.
details: Focus improves roll consistency before the action resolves.
Perfect rolls matter most against enemies that punish partial rolls or dodge weak hits.
Scholar can regain Focus through Refocus, making the class forgiving for new players.
steps.tip: Do not hoard Focus until the run collapses. Spend it before a Sanctum or Stone Hero, because those can refill the meter.

steps.title: Find the Sanctum and Stone Hero in Each Biome
steps.goal: Secure the local revive safety net and level boost before pushing into the next dangerous area.
details: Sanctum grants a useful buff and can protect a character from death once.
Stone Hero helps the party level quickly and refills Focus.
Scout the biome before rushing the main objective.
steps.tip: A party that skips free safety tools is just speedrunning regret.

steps.title: Treat Every Town as a Power Spike
steps.goal: Use towns for quests, gear, healing, mercenaries, and supplies before entering dungeons or story fights.
details: Check fresh town inventory on first visit.
Pick town quests with rewards that match your party needs.
Stock Godsbeard, Panax, and other survival items.
Hire mercenaries or followers when the party is underpowered.
steps.tip: Some early town quests are harsh for low-level parties. Pick rewards that solve an immediate weakness.

steps.title: Use Pull Rules to Control Fights
steps.goal: Enter battles on your terms instead of dragging the whole neighborhood into a disaster pile.
details: Dungeons pull every party member into the encounter.
Overworld fights can pull nearby allies and nearby enemies unless the party ambushes correctly.
Positioning before a fight can matter as much as the first attack roll.
steps.tip: Regroup before dungeons, split only when scouting is safe, and avoid starting fights beside extra enemies.

steps.title: Give Gear to the Stat That Actually Rolls It
steps.goal: Match weapons and utility items to the character whose core stat supports them.
details: Blacksmith: shields, defensive gear, and Strength tools.
Hunter: Awareness weapons and ranged damage upgrades.
Scholar: Intelligence tomes, staves, wands, and scroll synergy.
Herbalist: support gear, healing value, and Intelligence magic options.
Alchemist: Intelligence tools, bombs, and area damage utility.
steps.tip: Use the party summary and stat preview before handing out loot. A great weapon on the wrong character is just expensive furniture.

steps.title: Play Chapters as Progression, Not One Perfect Save
steps.goal: Use each run to unlock better starting options, learn enemy patterns, and improve future loadouts.
details: The campaign is split into separate adventures with roguelite progression.
Lore unlocks improve future starting options and loadout planning.
Dark Carnival offers an endless dungeon-style mode for repeatable challenge.
steps.tip: A failed run still teaches enemy behavior and helps build toward stronger future attempts.

quickTips: Spend Focus before using a Sanctum or Stone Hero, because those can refill the meter.
Scout each biome before rushing the main objective so free safety tools are not wasted.
Match weapons to the stat that actually rolls them instead of handing loot to whoever looks heroic.
Treat a failed run as progression: unlocks and learned patterns strengthen the next attempt.

forTheKingIIClassTierList.title: For The King II Class Tier List
forTheKingIIClassTierList.intro: Classes in For The King II are strongest when their stat, weapon type, and combat role match the party plan. This tier grid favors practical run value, beginner friendliness, and team utility over flashy chaos that looks clever right before the party dies.

tiers.label: Top Utility and Run Control
tiers.description: These classes solve major party problems and fit into most reliable team compositions.

classes.name: Herbalist
strengths: Party Heal gives strong group sustain.
Find Herb improves long-run supply value.
Can support the party in combat and during recovery phases.
classes.bestFor: New players, long campaigns, and safer co-op runs

classes.name: Alchemist
strengths: Find Potion can discover bombs while traveling.
Chemical attacks add strong area and burst options.
Works well when the party already has frontline protection.
classes.bestFor: Players who want higher burst, item utility, and flexible damage
classes.watchOut: Requires more item and timing awareness than starter classes.

tiers.label: Reliable Core Classes
tiers.description: These classes are easy to build around and remain useful across campaign and co-op play.

classes.name: Blacksmith
strengths: Starts with a shield-and-hammer tank identity.
Steadfast and Guard help protect the backline.
Excellent first pick for learning battle grid positioning.
classes.bestFor: Beginner parties, four-player co-op, and defensive team comps
classes.watchOut: Lower speed can delay key actions.

classes.name: Hunter
strengths: Elite Ambush and Elite Sneak help control overworld engagements.
Called Shot rewards perfect rolls with critical hits.
Strong ranged pressure from the back row.
classes.bestFor: Safe damage, ambush-focused play, and beginner-friendly ranged roles
classes.watchOut: Needs good Awareness weapons to keep damage scaling.

classes.name: Scholar
classes.role: Magic damage, control, and scroll economy
strengths: Uses tomes, staves, and wands well.
Find Scroll improves utility access.
Refocus can restore Focus at the end of a turn.
classes.bestFor: Magic damage, crowd control, and parties that spend Focus often

classes.name: Monk
strengths: Adds another support-oriented option beyond Herbalist.
Fits parties that want healing and utility without one support slot.
Works best in balanced groups that already have damage covered.
classes.bestFor: Support-heavy co-op groups and flexible campaign teams
classes.watchOut: Needs a clear party role to avoid overlapping with existing support.

classes.name: Fortune Teller
strengths: Elite Awareness profile suits ranged combat.
Tarot cards alter the flow of combat and party fortune.
Pairs well with parties that can protect a specialist backline.
classes.bestFor: DLC owners, Awareness-based teams, and tactical utility players
classes.watchOut: Less straightforward than standard damage classes.

tiers.label: Specialist Picks
tiers.description: These classes can be powerful, but they ask more from the player or need a specific party plan.

classes.name: Toymaker
strengths: Captures enemy essence into toys.
Can summon toys to reshape combat pressure.
Adds a unique playstyle built around preparation and tactical timing.
classes.bestFor: DLC owners, summon-focused teams, and experienced players
classes.watchOut: Needs planning and party protection to reach full value.

forTheKingIIBestPartyBuilds.title: For The King II Best Party Builds
forTheKingIIBestPartyBuilds.intro: The best For The King II party is not just four strong classes thrown into the same wagon of bad decisions. A good composition covers defense, damage types, healing, Focus usage, and battle grid positioning before the first dungeon starts collecting bodies.

builds.name: First Clear Balanced Party

roles.label: Frontline
roles.text: Blacksmith absorbs pressure and protects the row structure.

roles.label: Physical Damage
roles.text: Hunter handles ranged Awareness damage and safer overworld engagement.

roles.label: Magic Damage
roles.text: Scholar brings Intelligence-based magic, scroll utility, and Focus recovery.

roles.label: Support
roles.text: Herbalist supplies Party Heal and herb economy.

builds.gamePlan: Keep Blacksmith forward, protect Scholar and Herbalist, use Hunter to pick off priority targets, and spend Focus on rolls that prevent damage spikes.
lootPriority: Defensive shields and armor to Blacksmith
Awareness bows or ranged weapons to Hunter
Healing and support gear to Herbalist

builds.name: Developer Example Flex Party
bestFor: Groups that want utility over pure magic

roles.label: Frontline
roles.text: Blacksmith anchors the battle grid.

roles.label: Damage
roles.text: Hunter provides dependable ranged pressure.

roles.label: Support
roles.text: Herbalist keeps the party supplied and healed.

roles.label: Utility
roles.text: Busker adds a flexible fourth slot for support and party value.

builds.gamePlan: Use this as a stable co-op shell: one clear tank, one clear ranged attacker, one dedicated healer, and one utility slot that can adapt to the run.
lootPriority: Godsbeard and support items for Herbalist
Utility and economy gear for Busker

builds.name: Unlockable Burst Core
bestFor: Runs that need stronger burst and area damage

roles.label: Frontline
roles.text: Blacksmith gives the Alchemist time to set up damage.

roles.label: Ranged Damage
roles.text: Hunter handles consistent target pressure.

roles.label: Support
roles.text: Herbalist protects the party through longer fights.

roles.label: Burst Damage
roles.text: Alchemist adds bomb utility, chemical attacks, and explosive turns.

builds.gamePlan: Let Blacksmith and Herbalist stabilize the fight while Hunter and Alchemist remove high-value targets quickly.
lootPriority: Bomb and potion value to Alchemist

builds.name: Two-Player Co-op Split
bestFor: Local planning with two heroes per player

roles.label: Player One
roles.text: Blacksmith plus Herbalist controls survival, positioning, and healing decisions.

roles.label: Player Two
roles.text: Hunter plus Scholar controls ranged damage, magic pressure, and Focus spending.

builds.gamePlan: Split responsibilities so each player controls one defensive or support role and one active combat role. This keeps both players useful every turn instead of turning one person into the designated potion intern.
lootPriority: Player one prioritizes defense and healing stock
Player two prioritizes Awareness and Intelligence weapons

builds.name: Dark Carnival Safety Net
bestFor: Players who want sustain plus burst

roles.label: Survival
roles.text: Blacksmith and Herbalist reduce the chance of a fast collapse.

roles.label: Burst
roles.text: Alchemist handles explosive damage windows.

roles.label: Cleanup
roles.text: Hunter removes weakened or dangerous targets from range.

builds.gamePlan: Prioritize sustain and mixed damage. Dark Carnival rewards parties that can survive repeated rooms while still ending dangerous fights quickly.
lootPriority: Bomb and area damage tools second

builds.name: Tinkerers DLC Trickster Core
bestFor: Players who want a specialist party

roles.label: Frontline
roles.text: Blacksmith protects the specialist backline.

roles.label: Support
roles.text: Herbalist keeps the party stable.

roles.label: Summons
roles.text: Toymaker uses captured essence and toys to change combat pressure.

roles.label: Fate Control
roles.text: Fortune Teller adds Awareness-based pressure and tarot utility.

builds.gamePlan: Use Blacksmith and Herbalist to buy time while Toymaker and Fortune Teller create unusual tactical advantages.

forTheKingIILoreStoreCodes.title: For The King II Lore Store Codes and Unlocks
forTheKingIILoreStoreCodes.intro: For The King II uses the Lore Store as a long-term roguelite progression layer. The important distinction is simple: Lore unlocks are normal progression, Character Packs are official DLC content, and random third-party cheat shortcuts are not redeem codes.

cards.title: Public Lore Store Codes
cards.details: The Redeem Code field exists in-game, but regular public reward codes are not distributed as a standard For The King II feature. Special code use is reserved for cases such as official drops, competitions, or account support situations.

cards.title: Lore Currency
cards.details: Lore is earned through play and spent in the Lore Store to improve future run preparation. Unlocking new items and loadout options is part of normal campaign progression.

cards.title: Base-Game Class Unlocks
cards.details: For The King II launched with 12 playable classes, including returning roles such as Blacksmith, Hunter, Scholar, and Herbalist, plus new classes such as Alchemist and Stablehand.

cards.title: Free Update Character Content
cards.details: The Monk returned as part of the free Child of Chaos update, expanding support-focused party options beyond the original launch roster.

cards.title: Tinkerers of Fate Character Pack
cards.location: Steam DLC purchase and in-game progression
cards.details: This pack adds a Talent-based Toymaker who captures essence into toys and an Awareness-focused Fortune Teller who uses tarot cards to alter fortune.

cards.title: Fallen Oaths Character Pack
cards.location: Steam DLC purchase and in-game progression
cards.details: This pack expands the roster with the Fallen Knight and Voidwalker, plus themed weapon sets and gold-tier progression rewards.

cards.title: Into The Wild Character Pack
cards.location: Steam DLC purchase and in-game progression
cards.details: This pack adds the Beekeeper and Angler, with nature-themed class mechanics and new official unlockable content.

cards.title: Cheat Tools Are Not Codes
cards.details: Save edits, third-party unlock tools, trainers, and unofficial generators are not Lore Store codes and should not be presented as redeemable rewards.

forTheKingIICombatLoadouts.title: For The King II Combat and Loadouts Guide
forTheKingIICombatLoadouts.intro: For The King II combat is not just dice luck. Positioning, Focus spending, defense type, item timing, and loadout planning decide whether a fight is controlled or becomes another heroic group embarrassment.

items.label: Battle Grid Positioning
items.summary: Combat uses a 2x4 grid where position changes what characters can protect, target, avoid, or suffer from.
rules: Front-row characters can protect back-row allies with defensive tools such as Guard and Tower Shield style play.
Back-row characters are safer for ranged, magic, and support roles, but they are still vulnerable to row, column, and splash attacks.
Enemies also use the grid, so clumping the whole party together can invite area damage.
Movement can dodge hazards, protect fragile allies, or set up better lines for attacks.
items.bestUse: Put durable characters in front, keep fragile ranged or support characters behind them, and move before attacking when the grid position creates a better outcome.

items.label: AOE, Row, and Column Attacks
items.summary: Area attacks can hit more than one tile, which makes party spacing as important as raw damage.
rules: Row attacks can target every unit on a horizontal line.
Column attacks can hit front and back positions in the same vertical lane.
Magic weapons often use AOE patterns, but physical weapons can also have multi-target attacks.
Some special attacks cover larger or unusual shapes, so preview targeting before committing Focus.
items.bestUse: Spread the party before enemy AOE turns, then use your own row or column attacks when enemies line up badly.

items.label: Focus Spending
items.summary: Focus adds guaranteed rolls to combat checks, skill checks, and overworld movement.
rules: Spend Focus to secure key attacks, taunts, heals, AOE spells, and urgent movement.
Focus can be restored by Meditating in towns or camps, using Golden Root, or through traits such as Deep Thinker.
In dungeons, Focus is harder to restore because towns and camps are unavailable.
Before using a Sanctum or Stone Hero, spend spare Focus first because these points can refill the Focus meter.
items.bestUse: Save Focus for clutch actions, evasive targets, dangerous boss turns, and objectives where wasted movement would cost the party Chaos pressure.

items.label: Primary and Secondary Actions
items.summary: A turn usually ends after a primary action, while secondary actions handle movement and item use.
rules: Primary actions usually include attacking, using a major skill, or applying a combat ability.
Secondary actions can include moving, using herbs, drinking potions, throwing bombs, or using certain consumables.
The Nimble trait allows up to two secondary actions in a turn.
Dazed removes the next secondary action, which can block movement and item use at the worst possible time.
items.bestUse: Use secondary actions first when movement, items, or positioning can improve the primary action that follows.

items.label: Damage and Defense Types
items.summary: Physical Defence and Magical Resistance reduce different kinds of incoming damage.
rules: Physical Defence reduces physical damage.
Magical Resistance reduces magical damage.
Pierce bypasses armor and can punish heavily defended targets.
Weapon choice should match the user's best stat and the enemy's weaker defense type.
items.bestUse: Give Strength weapons to Strength characters, Intelligence weapons to casters, Talent weapons to Talent users, and keep at least one way to handle armored enemies.

items.label: Status Effects
items.summary: Status effects can remove turns, block attacks, lower stats, break equipment, or increase incoming damage.
rules: Dazed — disables the target's next secondary action, blocking item use and movement.
Stunned — skips the target's next turn.
Burn — deals fire damage over time after one turn and can come from burning tiles.
Entangled — prevents physical attacks and movement.
Lightning — makes one roll automatically fail.
Curse — greatly lowers one stat until cured.
Confusion — can trigger random actions, including using gear, consumables, or fleeing.
Poison — deals damage over time on the overworld and lowers stats.
Acid — can dissolve valuable gear permanently.
Fear — can force a character to flee and disrupt backline positioning.
Frozen — makes the target take extra damage.
Bleed — deals damage over time during combat.
items.bestUse: Carry cures for long routes and dungeons, because losing a turn or a weapon to a status effect is a very For The King II way to discover consequences.

items.label: Starting Loadouts and Gear Priority
items.summary: Loadout points, traits, weapons, armor, and consumables should be assigned by role instead of dumped randomly into whoever looks heroic.
rules: Starting loadouts can include improved weapons, armor, traits, and essential items unlocked through progression.
Front-row characters should get survivability first: armor, shields, defensive tools, and reliable taunt or guard options.
Damage dealers should get stat-matched weapons before decorative upgrades.
Support characters should carry herbs, Focus restoration, and items that protect the run from bad timing.
Gear and gold can be traded through the inventory when party members are close enough, and items can be passed through adjacent party members.
items.bestUse: Assign the best weapon to the character with the right stat, then distribute armor, cures, Golden Root, and healing items around the party before entering dungeons.

forTheKingIIMultiplayerCrossplay.title: For The King II Multiplayer and Crossplay Guide
forTheKingIIMultiplayerCrossplay.intro: For The King II supports solo play, online co-op, and local co-op, but platform labels matter. This comparison helps players know who can join before the party spends 20 minutes blaming the lobby.

rows.steam: Solo play and co-op with a party of up to four characters are supported.
rows.xbox: 1-4 players, with online co-op and local co-op listed for 2-4 players.
rows.playstation: 1-4 players listed, with online play for up to 4 players.
rows.note: A solo player controls the whole party. In co-op, players can control their own characters.

rows.steam: Online Co-op is listed on Steam. Hosts create an adventure from the multiplayer menu and can use region, room name, privacy, and password settings.
rows.xbox: Online co-op and online multiplayer are listed for 2-4 players.
rows.playstation: Online play supports up to 4 players and requires PlayStation Plus.
rows.note: Players need to join from the lobby before the host loads the save.

rows.steam: Shared or split-screen co-op is listed. Curve also describes offline co-op on the same PC with multiple-controller support.
rows.xbox: Xbox local co-op and local multiplayer are listed for 2-4 players.
rows.playstation: The PlayStation Store lists 1-4 players on PS4 and PS5.
rows.note: Offline co-op on the same PC can let multiple controllers input on everyone's turn.

rows.xbox: Xbox lists Xbox cross-platform multiplayer and Xbox cross-platform co-op.
rows.playstation: The PlayStation Store page lists online multiplayer and Remote Play support, without a cross-platform label.
rows.note: Mixed-platform parties should be planned around the store capability labels for the exact platform being used.

rows.feature: Remote Play, Cloud, and Ecosystem Features
rows.steam: Steam Remote Play Together is listed.
rows.xbox: Xbox Cloud Gaming, Xbox Play Anywhere, Smart Delivery, and Xbox Live cross-gen multiplayer are listed.
rows.playstation: Remote Play is supported. PS5 cloud streaming is listed with PlayStation Plus Premium.
rows.note: These features help when one player is remote, using another device, or playing across console and PC ecosystems.

rows.steam: Players cannot join a game already in progress. The host should save, quit to the adventure selector or campaign map, let everyone rejoin, then load the save.
rows.xbox: Use the same lobby-first rule when rebuilding a group before continuing a save.
rows.playstation: Use the same lobby-first rule when rebuilding a group before continuing a save.
rows.note: If a room does not appear, check region selection, game version, and whether the host is still inside an active run.

forTheKingIICampaignChapters.title: For The King II Campaign Chapters and Quests
forTheKingIICampaignChapters.intro: The campaign is a long chain of linked adventures, not a straight hallway with confetti. Players need to manage towns, quests, Sanctums, Stone Heroes, dungeons, and Chaos before the map starts collecting their mistakes.

steps.title: Start with the Seven-Adventure Campaign Structure
steps.summary: For The King II presents a campaign of over 30 hours split into seven narratively linked adventures.
details: Choose Apprentice, Journeyman, Master, or custom House Rules before the run begins.
Use House Rules to adjust Chaos Speed, Life Pool, Economy, and Loadout Points.
Treat failed runs as progression because unlocks and knowledge improve future starting loadouts.

steps.title: Follow the Named Story Adventure Spine
steps.summary: The story route pushes the party through Queen Rosomon's Fahrul across named adventures and escalating map objectives.
details: The Resistance — learn party roles, town shopping, combat positioning, and safe early quest choices.
The Primordial Oak — move efficiently between objectives and avoid wasting turns while Chaos pressure rises.
The Royal Mines — prepare for longer dungeon chains with healing, Focus recovery, and mixed damage types.
The Hangman's Noose — keep the party supplied, regroup before major pushes, and avoid entering fights underleveled.
The Castle Gates — commit saved resources, finalize weapons, and enter late objectives with cures, armor, and Focus tools.

steps.title: Clear Each Biome Before Rushing Forward
steps.summary: Each biome can contain key support points that make the next region safer.
details: Find the biome Sanctum before leaving the area.
Use Sanctums for powerful buffs and an extra revive safety net.
Find Stone Heroes because they help characters level up quickly.
Spend spare Focus before using a Sanctum or Stone Hero because these can refill the Focus meter.

steps.title: Use Towns for Quests, Gear, and Recovery
steps.summary: The first visit to a town gives fresh shops, quests, and possible mercenaries.
details: Check town goods before starting nearby fights.
Use town quests to gain useful rewards, but avoid early quests that pull the party into fights it cannot handle.
Hire mercenaries or followers early when the party needs more bodies, damage, or protection.
Meditate in towns or camps to restore Focus before major objectives.

steps.title: Control Enemy Pulls and Dungeon Entry
steps.summary: The overworld can drag nearby enemies into battle unless the party controls the approach.
details: Watch the red enemy radius before starting a fight.
Use ambushes to stop nearby enemies from joining.
Dungeons pull every party member in regardless of overworld distance.
Use dungeon entry to regroup, but do not enter without herbs, cures, Focus recovery, and repaired gear.

steps.title: Assign Rewards by Role, Not by Vibes
steps.summary: Loot choices matter because classes, traits, stats, and Battle Grid roles need different equipment.
details: Use Party Summary to check stats, buffs, debuffs, and immunities before assigning loot.
Use the loot preview information to decide who benefits most from a weapon or item.
Give front-line characters defensive gear first.
Give stat-matched weapons to the characters most likely to land reliable attacks.
Trade gear and gold through the inventory when characters are close enough, or pass items through nearby party members.

steps.title: Spend Focus and Movement Around Chaos Pressure
steps.summary: Focus can add movement spaces on the overworld, which makes it a campaign tool, not just a combat panic button.
details: Spend Focus to reach urgent objectives before Chaos pressure gets worse.
Save Focus in dungeons where recovery is limited.
Use Golden Root and Focus traits to keep key characters ready for difficult checks.
Avoid unnecessary side fights when the map timer is already punishing the party.

forTheKingIIDarkCarnival.title: For The King II Dark Carnival and Dungeon Crawl Guide
forTheKingIIDarkCarnival.intro: Dark Carnival and Dungeon Crawl give returning players fresh reasons to suffer productively: endless floors, seeded leaderboards, new bosses, new classes, and more ways to prove that planning beats optimism.

modes.name: Dark Carnival
modes.objective: Conquer as many floors as possible before the party falls.
mechanics: The mode is led by the Ringmaster.
Players collect Carnival Tickets to explore paths through the carnival.
Runs include themed rooms and unpredictable route choices.
The Wheel of Death can appear as part of the carnival's risk and reward structure.
preparation: Keep healing and Focus recovery distributed across the party.
Use AOE and crowd-control tools for long chains of fights.
Avoid spending all cures early because long-form dungeon modes punish sloppy item use.

modes.name: Dungeon Crawl
modes.objective: Push through a dungeon-focused roguelite run with randomized maps, bosses, encounters, and escalating threats.
mechanics: Dungeon Crawl returns as a free update for For The King II.
The mode uses randomly generated maps and rotating bosses.
It is built around deeper roguelite-style routing, dungeon pressure, and repeated combat preparation.
The update adds new bosses, enemies, weapons, cosmetics, traits, and companion content alongside the mode.
preparation: Use a party with both physical and magical damage.
Carry status cures before entering long dungeon chains.
Prioritize armor and survival on front-row characters.
Keep at least one character ready for Focus-heavy checks or clutch AOE turns.

modes.name: Dungeon Crawl Gauntlet
modes.objective: Compete on a seeded monthly map where players face the same layout, dungeon pressure, and boss structure.
mechanics: The Gauntlet format uses a seeded map for leaderboard competition.
Leaderboard runs reward consistency, routing, and clean combat more than lucky wandering.
Top leaderboard players can be honored with Stone Hero statue placements.
Because the seed is shared, route planning and party optimization become the main advantage.
preparation: Build a reliable team before chasing leaderboard attempts.
Record which routes, fights, and rewards produce the safest progress.
Use Focus on high-impact checks instead of low-value convenience rolls.
Treat every resource as part of the final score path.

modes.name: Into The Wild Character Pack
modes.objective: Add new playable classes, weapons, companion content, and cosmetic options to the base game.
mechanics: Adds the Angler class, built around fishing up treats, meals, riches, and movement-based combat mechanics.
Adds the Beekeeper class, built around sweet treats, stings, and a pet pollinator.
Adds Bludgeon and Harpoon weapon sets.
Includes a Gold-tier Trait, a new Merc, a new Pet, and a Cosmetic Skin.
preparation: Use the Angler when the party benefits from movement tricks and resource utility.
Use the Beekeeper when the party benefits from pet-driven pressure and support tools.
Test the new weapon sets against different front-row and back-row builds.
Pair new classes with stable defensive roles until their skill flow is familiar.

modes.name: Free Update Content
modes.objective: Refresh party building and dungeon routing with free additions released alongside the Dungeon Crawl update.
mechanics: The Treasure Hunter returns with new play options.
The Pathfinder receives updated abilities.
The update adds the Blunderbuss weapon set.
Bee and Bear enemies join the encounter pool.
Additional cosmetic, companion, and trait content expands run variety.
preparation: Recheck party roles after updates because new weapons and traits can change optimal builds.
Use the Encyclopedia and party summary tools to confirm unfamiliar mechanics.
Expect new enemies to punish old habits, because even game monsters believe in professional development.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about For The King II

questions.question: What is For The King II?
questions.answer: For The King II is a turn-based roguelite tabletop RPG with dice-inspired rolls, a Battle Grid combat system, procedurally generated Fahrul, and run-to-run progression through the Lore Store.

questions.question: Is For The King II multiplayer?
questions.answer: Yes. For The King II supports solo play, online co-op, and local co-op for up to four players, with one player controlling the whole party in solo mode.

questions.question: What platforms is For The King II on?
questions.answer: For The King II is available on Steam for PC, Xbox, and PlayStation. Xbox lists cross-platform multiplayer and Xbox Play Anywhere support.

questions.question: Does For The King II support crossplay?
questions.answer: Steam and Xbox list cross-platform multiplayer labels for For The King II. PlayStation lists online multiplayer and Remote Play, so mixed-platform parties should follow each store's capability labels.

questions.question: Are there active redeem codes in For The King II?
questions.answer: For The King II has an in-game Redeem Code field, but it does not distribute a public freebie code list. Long-term rewards come through the Lore Store, free updates, and official character packs.

questions.question: How many classes are in For The King II?
questions.answer: For The King II launched with 12 playable classes and adds more through free updates like the Monk and paid character packs such as Tinkerers of Fate, Fallen Oaths, and Into The Wild.

questions.question: What is the Lore Store in For The King II?
questions.answer: The Lore Store is For The King II's roguelite progression layer. Lore earned through play unlocks new starting loadouts, weapons, cosmetics, and class options that strengthen future runs.

questions.question: Does For The King II have an endgame mode?
questions.answer: Yes. For The King II includes the Dark Carnival infinite dungeon and the free Dungeon Crawl update with randomized maps, rotating bosses, and a seeded Gauntlet leaderboard.

cta.description: Join the For The King II community on Discord for guides, updates, and discussions!
